roadmap.title: 45-Day Data Science Roadmap
roadmap.description: A structured 45-day beginner curriculum to become a Data Scientist - covering Python, statistics, data wrangling, visualization, SQL, machine learning, communication, and a deployed capstone project.
roadmap.outcome: Ability to collect, clean, analyze, and model data, communicate insights, and build and share end-to-end data science projects.

days.title: What is Data Science and the Data Scientist Role
objectives: Understand what data science is and is not
Know the tools and skills of a data scientist
topics: Definition and scope of data science
Data science vs data analytics vs ML engineering
days.beginner_explanation: Data science is the practice of extracting insights and building predictions from data to support decisions. A data scientist blends three skill sets: statistics to reason about data, programming to manipulate it, and domain and communication skills to turn results into action. Before writing any code, it helps to understand the full lifecycle - from framing a business question, gathering and cleaning data, exploring and modeling it, to communicating findings. Knowing this map keeps you oriented as you learn each individual skill over the coming weeks.
days.practical_task: Research three real data science job postings and list the tools, skills, and responsibilities they share. Map each to a stage of the data science lifecycle.
days.mini_project: Create a one-page written overview of a data science problem in a domain you like (e.g., sports, healthcare), describing the question, data needed, and how you would approach it.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between data science, data analytics, and machine learning engineering in your own words with an example of each.

quiz.question: Which best describes the FIRST stage of a data science project?
options: Framing the business problem and questions
quiz.answer: Framing the business problem and questions

quiz.question: Which is an example of unstructured data?
quiz.answer: Free-text customer reviews

research_papers.title: 50 Years of Data Science (Donoho, 2017)

days.expected_outcome: You understand what data science is and how a data scientist works end to end.

days.title: Python Fundamentals for Data Science
objectives: Control flow with conditionals and loops
days.beginner_explanation: Python is the dominant language in data science because it is readable, powerful, and has a huge ecosystem of data libraries. Before those libraries make sense, you need the basics: storing values in variables, understanding data types, making decisions with conditionals, and repeating work with loops. These building blocks appear in every data script you will write, from cleaning a dataset to training a model. Mastering them now makes everything that follows far easier.
days.practical_task: Write Python scripts that compute statistics on a list of numbers (sum, average, max) using loops and conditionals, without any libraries.
days.mini_project: Build a simple text-based data quiz or number-guessing game that uses loops, conditionals, and user input to reinforce control flow.
days.assignment: Write a program that classifies a list of exam scores into grade buckets (A/B/C/F) using conditionals and a loop, and prints a count of each grade.

quiz.question: What does a for loop do?
options: Repeats a block over a sequence
quiz.answer: Repeats a block over a sequence

quiz.question: Which data type would you use for True/False values?
quiz.answer: bool

research_papers.title: Python for Scientific Computing (Oliphant, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can write basic Python programs using variables, conditionals, and loops.

days.title: Python Data Structures and Functions
objectives: Use lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets
days.beginner_explanation: Data structures are how you organize data in memory, and choosing the right one makes your code cleaner and faster. Lists hold ordered collections, dictionaries map keys to values (perfect for structured records), sets store unique items, and tuples are immutable groupings. Functions let you package logic for reuse, and comprehensions provide a concise way to transform data. These tools are the everyday vocabulary of data manipulation and prepare you to understand how libraries like Pandas organize data.
days.practical_task: Given a list of dictionaries representing people, write functions to filter, sort, and summarize them using comprehensions and built-in functions.
days.mini_project: Build a small contact book or inventory manager in pure Python using dictionaries and functions, supporting add, search, and summary operations.
days.assignment: Write a function that takes a list of words and returns a dictionary of word frequencies, using a comprehension or loop.

quiz.question: Which structure maps keys to values?
quiz.answer: dictionary

quiz.question: What does a function's return statement do?
options: Sends a value back to the caller
quiz.answer: Sends a value back to the caller

research_papers.title: Python for Scientific Computing (Oliphant, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can organize and manipulate data using Python's core data structures and functions.

days.title: Data Science Environment and Tools
objectives: Set up a data science environment
days.beginner_explanation: Data scientists live in interactive environments that mix code, output, and notes. Jupyter notebooks let you run code in cells, see results immediately, and document your thinking with Markdown - ideal for exploration and sharing analyses. Managing packages with pip or conda and isolating projects in virtual environments prevents the classic 'it works on my machine' problems. Setting this up properly now gives you a smooth, reproducible workspace for every project ahead.
days.practical_task: Create a virtual environment, install NumPy and Pandas, and build a Jupyter notebook that documents a small analysis with Markdown headings and code cells.
days.mini_project: Set up a clean, reproducible project folder with a virtual environment, a requirements file, and a well-documented starter notebook.
days.assignment: Write a short notebook explaining, with Markdown and code cells, why virtual environments matter and demonstrate creating one.

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of a virtual environment?
quiz.answer: Isolating project dependencies

quiz.question: Jupyter notebooks are especially good for:
options: Interactive analysis mixing code, output, and notes
quiz.answer: Interactive analysis mixing code, output, and notes

research_papers.title: Jupyter Notebooks - a publishing format for reproducible computational workflows (Kluyver et al., 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can set up and work in a reproducible data science environment with Jupyter.

days.title: NumPy for Numerical Computing
days.beginner_explanation: NumPy is the foundation of the entire Python data stack. It provides the ndarray, a fast, memory-efficient array that supports vectorized operations - applying an operation to a whole array at once instead of looping. This is both faster and cleaner than plain Python. Pandas, scikit-learn, and deep learning libraries all build on NumPy, so understanding arrays, indexing, and broadcasting gives you the numerical fluency that underpins all quantitative data work.
days.practical_task: Create NumPy arrays, use boolean masking to filter values, compute summary statistics, and demonstrate broadcasting with arrays of different shapes.
days.mini_project: Analyze a numeric dataset (e.g., temperatures or sales) purely with NumPy: load values, compute statistics, normalize them, and find outliers using masks.
days.assignment: Explain vectorization with an example comparing a Python loop and a NumPy operation, and describe why NumPy is faster.

quiz.question: What is a key advantage of NumPy arrays over Python lists?
quiz.answer: Fast vectorized numerical operations

quiz.question: Boolean masking is used to:
quiz.answer: Filter elements meeting a condition

research_papers.title: Array Programming with NumPy (Harris et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can perform efficient numerical computations with NumPy arrays.

days.title: Pandas Fundamentals
days.beginner_explanation: Pandas is the workhorse of data science - it lets you work with tabular data (rows and columns) the way you would in a spreadsheet, but programmatically and at scale. The DataFrame is its central object. You will constantly load data from files, inspect its shape and summary statistics, select and filter the rows and columns you care about, and sort results. These operations are the daily bread of any data scientist and the gateway to cleaning and analysis.
days.practical_task: Load a real CSV dataset into a DataFrame, inspect it with head/info/describe, filter rows by conditions, and select specific columns.
days.mini_project: Perform a first-pass exploration of a public dataset (e.g., Titanic or a sales CSV): load it, summarize it, and answer five simple questions with filtering and sorting.
days.assignment: Load a dataset and write code to answer: how many rows, what are the column types, and which rows meet a specific condition you choose.

quiz.question: A Pandas DataFrame is best described as:
options: A 2D labeled table of rows and columns
quiz.answer: A 2D labeled table of rows and columns

quiz.question: Which method gives summary statistics of numeric columns?
quiz.answer: df.describe()

youtube.title: Pandas Tutorial for Beginners - Full Course

research_papers.title: Data Structures for Statistical Computing in Python (McKinney, 2010)

days.expected_outcome: You can load, inspect, and query tabular datasets with Pandas.

days.title: Advanced Pandas: Grouping, Merging, and Reshaping
objectives: Combine datasets with merge and concat
Reshape data with pivot and melt
days.beginner_explanation: Real analysis rarely uses a single flat table. You need to summarize data by categories (groupby), combine information from multiple sources (merge/join), and reshape data between wide and long formats for different purposes. These operations turn raw tables into the aggregated, joined datasets that answer business questions - like average revenue per region or combining customer and order tables. Mastering them is what lets you actually extract insight rather than just view rows.
days.practical_task: Use groupby to compute aggregate statistics by category, merge two related DataFrames on a key, and create a pivot table summarizing the result.
days.mini_project: Combine two related datasets (e.g., orders and customers), then produce grouped summaries and a pivot table answering business questions about the merged data.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between an inner and a left join with a small example, and show a groupby that computes a metric per category.

quiz.question: groupby is used to:
options: Split data into groups and aggregate
quiz.answer: Split data into groups and aggregate

quiz.question: A left join keeps:
options: All rows from the left table plus matches from the right
quiz.answer: All rows from the left table plus matches from the right

research_papers.title: Tidy Data (Wickham, 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You can aggregate, combine, and reshape datasets to answer analytical questions.

days.title: Data Cleaning and Preparation
days.beginner_explanation: Real-world data is messy: missing values, duplicates, wrong types, and inconsistent formats are everywhere. Data cleaning is often the largest part of a data scientist's job, and the quality of your cleaning directly determines the quality of your analysis - garbage in, garbage out. You will learn systematic ways to find and fix these issues so that your downstream statistics and models are trustworthy. Clean data is the unglamorous but essential foundation of every reliable result.
days.practical_task: Take a messy dataset, identify missing values and duplicates, decide how to handle each, fix data types, and standardize inconsistent text fields.
days.mini_project: Build a documented data-cleaning notebook that turns a raw, messy public dataset into a clean, analysis-ready version, explaining each decision.
days.assignment: Given a dataset with missing values, describe two strategies for handling them and explain when each is appropriate.

quiz.question: Why is data cleaning important?
options: Poor data quality leads to unreliable analysis and models
quiz.answer: Poor data quality leads to unreliable analysis and models

quiz.question: One common way to handle missing numeric values is:
options: Impute with the mean or median
quiz.answer: Impute with the mean or median

research_papers.title: Tidy Data (Wickham, 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You can turn messy raw data into clean, reliable, analysis-ready datasets.

days.title: Descriptive Statistics for Data Science
objectives: Understand measures of central tendency and dispersion
Recognize the shape and spread of distributions
days.beginner_explanation: Descriptive statistics summarize a dataset with a few numbers so you can understand it without reading every row. Central tendency (mean, median, mode) tells you where the 'middle' of your data is: the mean is the average, the median is the middle value when sorted (robust to outliers), and the mode is the most frequent value. Dispersion tells you how spread out the data is: variance and standard deviation measure average distance from the mean, while the IQR (interquartile range) captures the middle 50% and is used to detect outliers. Percentiles rank values (the 90th percentile is the value below which 90% of data falls). Skewness measures asymmetry and kurtosis measures tail-heaviness. Together these numbers let a data scientist quickly grasp a variable's typical value, variability, and outliers before doing deeper analysis.
days.practical_task: Take a numeric dataset (e.g., Titanic fares or house prices) and compute mean, median, mode, std, variance, and quartiles using Pandas .describe() and manual calculations; interpret what each number says about the data.
days.mini_project: Build a 'Dataset Summary Report' function that takes any DataFrame and prints a formatted statistical summary for every numeric column, flagging columns that are highly skewed or contain outliers based on the IQR rule.
days.assignment: Choose 3 numeric columns from a real dataset, compute the full five-number summary for each, and write 2-3 sentences per column explaining what the statistics reveal.

quiz.question: Which measure of central tendency is most robust to outliers?
quiz.answer: Median

quiz.question: What does the IQR represent?
quiz.answer: The middle 50% of the data

youtube.title: Statistics - A Full University Course on Data Science Basics

research_papers.title: The Visual Display of Quantitative Information (Tufte)

days.expected_outcome: You can summarize any numeric dataset with the right statistics and interpret what they mean.

days.title: Data Visualization with Matplotlib
objectives: Create core plot types with Matplotlib
Choose the right chart for the data
topics: Line, bar, scatter, and histogram plots
days.beginner_explanation: Matplotlib is the foundational plotting library in Python, and almost every other visualization tool builds on it. It uses a Figure (the whole canvas) containing one or more Axes (individual plots). You choose a chart type based on your question: line plots show trends over time, bar charts compare categories, scatter plots reveal relationships between two numeric variables, and histograms show the distribution of a single variable. Good visualization is about communication, not decoration, so you always add clear titles, axis labels, and legends so a viewer can understand the chart without explanation. Learning to control the Figure/Axes structure lets you build multi-panel dashboards and precisely customize every element. Visualization is one of a data scientist's most important skills because a well-chosen chart can reveal patterns, outliers, and trends that summary statistics alone would hide.
days.practical_task: Using a dataset of your choice, create a line plot, bar chart, scatter plot, and histogram, each with proper titles, axis labels, and legends, then arrange four of them in a 2x2 subplot grid.
days.mini_project: Build a visual EDA notebook for a dataset (e.g., Gapminder or Titanic) that uses at least five different Matplotlib chart types to tell a story about the data.
days.assignment: Recreate a chart you find in a news article or report using Matplotlib, matching the chart type and labeling as closely as you can.

quiz.question: Which chart type is best for showing the distribution of a single numeric variable?
quiz.answer: Histogram

quiz.question: In Matplotlib, what contains the individual plot with its axes and data?
quiz.answer: Axes

research_papers.title: Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment (Hunter, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can create clear, well-labeled charts to explore and communicate data.

days.title: Advanced Visualization with Seaborn
objectives: Create statistical visualizations quickly with Seaborn
days.beginner_explanation: Seaborn is a high-level visualization library built on top of Matplotlib that makes attractive statistical charts with far less code. Where Matplotlib requires many lines to build a styled plot, Seaborn produces it in one line and works directly with Pandas DataFrames. It excels at statistical visualization: KDE plots show smooth distributions, box and violin plots compare distributions across categories, and regplot adds a regression line to a scatter plot automatically. Two especially powerful tools for exploratory analysis are the correlation heatmap (which shows how strongly numeric variables relate to each other using color) and the pairplot (which plots every pair of variables in a grid). Seaborn also ships with polished themes and color palettes, so your charts look professional by default. Most data scientists use Seaborn for fast exploration and Matplotlib underneath for fine-grained customization.
days.practical_task: On a dataset with several numeric and categorical columns, create a correlation heatmap, a pairplot, a boxplot grouped by a category, and a regplot, then interpret the relationships you see.
days.mini_project: Build a Seaborn-based exploratory analysis of a dataset (e.g., tips, iris, or penguins) that uses at least six different Seaborn plot types and includes written interpretations of each finding.
days.assignment: Create a correlation heatmap for a dataset and write a short paragraph identifying the two strongest and two weakest relationships.

quiz.question: What is Seaborn primarily built on top of?
quiz.answer: Matplotlib

quiz.question: Which Seaborn plot shows all pairwise relationships between numeric variables at once?
quiz.answer: pairplot

research_papers.title: seaborn: statistical data visualization (Waskom, 2021)

days.expected_outcome: You can rapidly produce polished statistical visualizations and read relationships between variables.

days.title: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
days.beginner_explanation: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the detective phase of data science where you investigate a dataset to understand its structure, spot problems, and discover patterns before building models. A systematic EDA moves through three levels: univariate analysis looks at one variable at a time (its distribution, central tendency, and outliers), bivariate analysis examines relationships between two variables (like how price relates to size), and multivariate analysis considers several variables together. Throughout, you're asking questions: Are there missing values or outliers? Which variables are correlated? Are there surprising groupings or trends? Good EDA combines summary statistics with visualization because charts reveal patterns numbers can hide. Tools like ydata-profiling can auto-generate a full report, but the real skill is asking the right questions and forming hypotheses that guide the rest of the project. EDA is where most of a data scientist's insight and intuition is built.
days.practical_task: Perform a complete EDA on an unfamiliar dataset: examine each variable's distribution, check for missing values and outliers, explore correlations, and write down at least five insights you discover.
days.mini_project: Create a full EDA report notebook on a real dataset (e.g., a Kaggle dataset) that includes data overview, univariate/bivariate/multivariate analysis, outlier detection, and a summary of key findings and hypotheses.
days.assignment: Run ydata-profiling on a dataset and compare its automated report to your own manual EDA, noting what the tool missed that your judgment caught.

quiz.question: What does bivariate analysis examine?
quiz.answer: The relationship between two variables

quiz.question: What is the main goal of EDA?
options: To understand the data and form hypotheses
quiz.answer: To understand the data and form hypotheses

youtube.title: Exploratory Data Analysis with Pandas Python

research_papers.title: Exploratory Data Analysis (Tukey, 1977)

days.expected_outcome: You can systematically explore any dataset and extract meaningful insights and hypotheses.

days.title: Probability Fundamentals
days.beginner_explanation: Probability is the mathematics of uncertainty, and it underlies nearly all of statistics and machine learning. It starts with a sample space (all possible outcomes) and events (subsets of outcomes we care about). The basic rules let you combine probabilities: the addition rule handles 'or' situations and the multiplication rule handles 'and' situations for independent events. Conditional probability, written P(A|B), asks how likely A is given that B already happened, and it captures how information updates our beliefs. Independence means one event doesn't affect another's probability. Bayes' theorem formalizes how to flip conditional probabilities and update beliefs with new evidence, which is the foundation for classifiers like Naive Bayes and for Bayesian thinking generally. Random variables assign numbers to outcomes, and expectation is the long-run average value. Understanding probability gives you the vocabulary to reason correctly about randomness, risk, and the models you'll build later.
days.practical_task: Simulate coin flips and dice rolls with NumPy to empirically verify probability rules, and compute a conditional probability from a real two-way table (e.g., survival by class in Titanic).
days.mini_project: Build a small 'probability simulator' that estimates probabilities of compound events (e.g., getting at least one six in four dice rolls) via simulation and compares them to the theoretical values.
days.assignment: Work through three Bayes' theorem word problems (e.g., a medical test scenario) by hand, then verify your answers with a simulation.

quiz.question: What does P(A|B) represent?
quiz.answer: Probability of A given B

quiz.question: If two events are independent, then P(A and B) equals:
quiz.answer: P(A) x P(B)

research_papers.title: An Essay towards Solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances (Bayes, 1763)

days.expected_outcome: You can reason about uncertainty and apply probability rules to data problems.

days.title: Probability Distributions and Sampling
objectives: Grasp sampling and the Central Limit Theorem
topics: The normal distribution and the 68-95-99.7 rule
days.beginner_explanation: Probability distributions describe how likely different values of a random variable are. Discrete distributions (like the binomial, for counts of successes, and the Poisson, for counts of events in a time window) apply to countable outcomes, while continuous distributions (like the normal) apply to measurements. The normal (bell-curve) distribution is central to statistics: it's symmetric around its mean, and the 68-95-99.7 rule says about 68% of values fall within one standard deviation, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three. Sampling is the act of drawing a subset from a population, and a sampling distribution describes how a statistic (like the sample mean) varies across many samples. The Central Limit Theorem is the powerful result that the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size grows, regardless of the population's shape. This is why so many statistical methods assume normality and why the normal distribution appears everywhere in data science.
days.practical_task: Use scipy.stats and NumPy to generate and plot normal, binomial, and Poisson distributions, then demonstrate the Central Limit Theorem by repeatedly sampling from a skewed distribution and plotting the distribution of sample means.
days.mini_project: Build an interactive notebook that lets you vary distribution parameters (mean, std, n) and visualizes how the shape changes, plus a demonstration of the CLT with adjustable sample sizes.
days.assignment: Given a real dataset column, determine which distribution best describes it and justify your choice with a histogram overlaid with the theoretical distribution.

quiz.question: According to the 68-95-99.7 rule, what percent of data falls within 2 standard deviations of the mean in a normal distribution?
The Central Limit Theorem states that the distribution of sample means approaches which shape as sample size grows?
quiz.answer: Normal

research_papers.title: The Central Limit Theorem: A Historical Note

days.expected_outcome: You understand key distributions and why the normal distribution and CLT matter for statistics.

days.title: Inferential Statistics and Hypothesis Testing
objectives: Understand statistical inference and confidence intervals
topics: Population vs sample and point estimates
t-tests, chi-square tests, and ANOVA basics
Type I and Type II errors
days.beginner_explanation: Inferential statistics lets you draw conclusions about a whole population from a sample. A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a population parameter (e.g., 'the average is between 48 and 52 with 95% confidence') and communicates the uncertainty of an estimate. Hypothesis testing is a formal procedure for deciding whether an observed effect is real or could be due to chance. You state a null hypothesis (no effect) and an alternative (there is an effect), then compute a p-value: the probability of seeing data at least as extreme as yours if the null were true. If the p-value is below a chosen significance level (commonly 0.05), you reject the null. Different tests suit different questions: t-tests compare means, chi-square tests examine categorical associations, and ANOVA compares means across several groups. It's crucial to understand errors: a Type I error is a false positive (rejecting a true null) and a Type II error is a false negative. This framework is the backbone of A/B testing and scientific data analysis.
days.practical_task: Use scipy.stats to run a t-test comparing two groups (e.g., fares of survivors vs non-survivors), compute a confidence interval, and correctly interpret the p-value in plain language.
days.mini_project: Design and analyze a simulated A/B test: generate data for a control and treatment group, run the appropriate hypothesis test, report the p-value and confidence interval, and write a clear conclusion.
days.assignment: Find a claim in a news article that reports a statistic and critique it in terms of sample size, significance, and possible errors.

quiz.question: What is a p-value?
options: The probability the null hypothesis is true
The probability of observing data at least as extreme as yours if the null is true
quiz.answer: The probability of observing data at least as extreme as yours if the null is true

quiz.question: Rejecting a null hypothesis that is actually true is which type of error?
quiz.answer: Type I error

research_papers.title: The ASA Statement on p-Values (Wasserstein & Lazar, 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can run and correctly interpret hypothesis tests and confidence intervals.

days.title: Correlation and Simple Linear Regression
objectives: Understand and fit simple linear regression
days.beginner_explanation: Correlation measures how strongly two numeric variables move together, on a scale from -1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 meaning no linear relationship. Pearson correlation captures linear relationships while Spearman captures monotonic ones based on ranks. A critical lesson is that correlation does not imply causation: two variables can move together because of coincidence or a hidden third factor. Simple linear regression goes a step further by fitting a straight line (y = mx + b) that best predicts one variable from another, using the least squares method to minimize the total squared distance between the line and the data points. R-squared tells you the proportion of variance in the outcome explained by the predictor, and residuals (the leftover errors) help you check whether the model fits well. This is the gateway to machine learning: regression is both a statistical tool for understanding relationships and the simplest predictive model you'll build.
days.practical_task: Compute Pearson and Spearman correlations for pairs of variables, fit a simple linear regression with scikit-learn or statsmodels, plot the regression line, and interpret the slope and R-squared.
days.mini_project: Build a regression analysis on a real dataset (e.g., predicting tips from bill amount) that includes a scatter plot with fitted line, correlation coefficients, R-squared, and a residual plot with interpretation.
days.assignment: Find two variables that are correlated but not causally related and write a short explanation of the likely confounding factor.

quiz.question: A correlation coefficient of -0.9 indicates:
quiz.answer: A strong negative relationship

quiz.question: What does R-squared measure?
options: The proportion of variance explained by the model
quiz.answer: The proportion of variance explained by the model

research_papers.title: Regression Towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature (Galton, 1886)

days.expected_outcome: You can measure and interpret relationships between variables and fit a simple regression model.

days.title: SQL for Data Science - Fundamentals
objectives: Query data with core SQL statements
topics: Aggregate functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX)
days.beginner_explanation: SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for working with data stored in relational databases, and it's one of the most in-demand skills for data scientists because most organizational data lives in databases, not CSV files. A relational database organizes data into tables (rows and columns) that can relate to each other. The SELECT statement retrieves columns, WHERE filters rows by conditions, ORDER BY sorts results, and LIMIT restricts how many rows come back. Aggregate functions summarize data: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, and MAX. GROUP BY splits data into groups so you can aggregate within each (e.g., average salary per department), and HAVING filters those groups after aggregation. Handling NULLs correctly matters because missing values behave specially in SQL. Mastering these fundamentals lets you pull exactly the data you need directly from the source, often far more efficiently than loading everything into Python first.
days.practical_task: Using a sample database (e.g., SQLite Chinook or a Kaggle SQL dataset), write queries to filter rows, sort results, and compute grouped aggregates like counts and averages per category.
days.mini_project: Build a set of 10 analytical SQL queries answering business questions about a sample database, ranging from simple filters to grouped aggregations with HAVING clauses.
days.assignment: Write five queries that each use GROUP BY with a different aggregate function and explain in one sentence what business question each answers.

quiz.question: Which SQL clause is used to filter groups after aggregation?
quiz.answer: HAVING

quiz.question: Which function counts the number of rows?
quiz.answer: COUNT()

youtube.title: SQL Tutorial - Full Database Course for Beginners

research_papers.title: A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks (Codd, 1970)

days.expected_outcome: You can retrieve, filter, and summarize data directly from a relational database with SQL.

days.title: SQL for Data Science - Joins and Subqueries
objectives: Combine data from multiple tables with joins
topics: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL joins
days.beginner_explanation: Real databases spread data across many related tables, so the ability to combine them with joins is essential. An INNER JOIN keeps only rows that match in both tables, a LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table (filling in NULLs where there's no match), and RIGHT and FULL joins do the mirror and union cases. Subqueries are queries nested inside other queries, useful for filtering based on a computed set or value. Common Table Expressions (CTEs), written with the WITH keyword, let you name intermediate results to make complex queries readable and reusable. Window functions are a powerful analytical tool that compute values across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them, enabling running totals, rankings, and moving averages, which are hard to do otherwise. Finally, Pandas' read_sql lets you run these queries from Python and load results straight into a DataFrame, bridging the database and your analysis. Together these skills let you assemble and analyze data at the source like a professional.
days.practical_task: Write queries that join at least three tables, use a CTE to structure a multi-step analysis, and apply a window function to rank items within groups; then load one result into Pandas with read_sql.
days.mini_project: Build an analytical report from a multi-table database that uses joins, a CTE, and window functions to answer a layered business question (e.g., top-selling product per region with running totals).
days.assignment: Rewrite a complex nested subquery as a CTE and explain why the CTE version is easier to read and maintain.

quiz.question: Which join returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right?
quiz.answer: LEFT JOIN

quiz.question: What keyword introduces a Common Table Expression?
quiz.answer: WITH

research_papers.title: Access Path Selection in a Relational Database Management System (Selinger et al., 1979)

days.expected_outcome: You can combine and analyze data across multiple tables using advanced SQL.

days.title: Data Collection: APIs and Web Scraping
objectives: Scrape data from web pages responsibly
Understand data collection ethics and limits
days.beginner_explanation: Not all data comes in a tidy CSV; often you must collect it yourself from the web. There are two main approaches. APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are the preferred way: many services expose endpoints that return structured data, usually as JSON, and you fetch it with HTTP requests using Python's requests library. You'll learn about HTTP methods (GET to retrieve), status codes, query parameters, authentication with API keys, pagination (fetching results page by page), and rate limits (how often you're allowed to call). When no API exists, web scraping extracts data directly from HTML pages using tools like BeautifulSoup to parse the page structure and pull out the elements you need. Scraping must be done responsibly: you should check a site's robots.txt file, respect terms of service, avoid overloading servers, and never collect personal data unlawfully. These skills let a data scientist build original datasets that others don't have, which is often where the most valuable insights come from.

days.title: Feature Engineering
objectives: Create informative features from raw data
Encode categorical and scale numeric features
Understand the impact of features on models
topics: What features are and why they matter
Creating features from dates and text
days.beginner_explanation: Feature engineering is the craft of transforming raw data into inputs that help machine learning models learn better, and it's often what separates a mediocre model from a great one. A 'feature' is any measurable input variable. Models generally need numeric input, so categorical variables must be encoded: one-hot encoding creates a binary column per category (good for unordered categories), label/ordinal encoding maps categories to numbers (appropriate when there's a natural order). Numeric features often benefit from scaling (standardization to mean 0 and std 1, or min-max normalization to a 0-1 range) so that no single feature dominates just because of its units. You can also create new features that expose hidden signal: extracting the day-of-week or month from a date, computing ratios, binning continuous values into ranges, or building interaction terms that combine two variables. Thoughtful feature engineering uses domain knowledge to give the model the most informative view of the problem, and it frequently improves accuracy more than switching algorithms.
days.practical_task: Take a dataset with categorical, numeric, and date columns and engineer a richer feature set: one-hot encode categories, scale numeric columns, and extract at least three new features from dates or combinations.
days.mini_project: Build a feature engineering pipeline for a dataset (e.g., a housing or e-commerce dataset) that produces a model-ready feature matrix, documenting the rationale for each transformation.

days.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
objectives: Understand what machine learning is and its main types
topics: Supervised vs unsupervised vs reinforcement learning
days.beginner_explanation: Machine learning is the practice of building systems that learn patterns from data to make predictions or decisions, rather than being explicitly programmed with rules. There are three broad types: supervised learning, where the model learns from labeled examples (inputs paired with known outputs) to predict labels for new data; unsupervised learning, where the model finds structure in unlabeled data (like grouping similar customers); and reinforcement learning, where an agent learns by trial and error through rewards. Supervised problems split into classification (predicting a category, like spam or not-spam) and regression (predicting a number, like price). The core workflow is to split your data into a training set (used to fit the model) and a test set (held back to estimate how well the model generalizes to unseen data), because a model that memorizes training data but fails on new data is useless. Scikit-learn provides a consistent API for all of this: every model has .fit() to train and .predict() to make predictions, which makes experimenting with different algorithms easy.

days.title: Linear and Logistic Regression
days.beginner_explanation: Linear and logistic regression are the two workhorse models every data scientist must know because they are simple, fast, and highly interpretable. Multiple linear regression extends the single-predictor line to many features, predicting a numeric outcome as a weighted sum of inputs; each coefficient tells you how much the outcome changes when that feature increases by one unit, holding others constant. It relies on assumptions (linearity, independence, constant variance of errors) that you check with diagnostics. Logistic regression, despite its name, is a classification model: it passes a linear combination of features through the sigmoid function, which squashes any number into a probability between 0 and 1, then classifies based on a threshold (usually 0.5). Its coefficients relate to the odds of the positive class, so they're interpretable too. These models are often the first thing to try on a new problem: they set a strong baseline, run instantly, and their transparency helps you understand which features actually matter.

days.title: Decision Trees and k-Nearest Neighbors
objectives: Understand how decision trees make predictions
days.beginner_explanation: Decision trees and k-Nearest Neighbors are intuitive models that work very differently from regression. A decision tree makes predictions by asking a series of yes/no questions about the features, splitting the data at each node to make the resulting groups as 'pure' as possible (measured by Gini impurity or entropy), until it reaches a leaf that gives the prediction. Trees are easy to visualize and require no feature scaling, but they tend to overfit: a deep tree can memorize the training data and fail on new data, which is why you limit depth or prune the tree. k-Nearest Neighbors is even simpler in concept: to classify a new point, it looks at the k closest training points (by a distance metric like Euclidean distance) and takes a majority vote. It's a 'lazy' learner that stores all the data and does the work at prediction time. Choosing k matters: too small and it's noisy, too large and it blurs the boundaries. Both models help you build intuition for how different algorithms carve up the feature space.
days.practical_task: Train a decision tree and a k-NN classifier on the same dataset, visualize the tree, experiment with tree depth and k values, and observe how each affects training vs test accuracy.

quiz.answer: The model becomes noisy and sensitive to outliers

days.title: Model Evaluation and Validation
days.beginner_explanation: Building a model is easy; knowing whether it's actually good is the real skill, and that's what model evaluation is about. For classification, accuracy (percent correct) can be misleading on imbalanced data, so you use precision (of the items predicted positive, how many really are), recall (of the actual positives, how many you caught), and their harmonic mean, the F1 score. The confusion matrix breaks predictions into true/false positives and negatives, and ROC-AUC summarizes performance across all thresholds. For regression, you measure error with MAE (average absolute error), MSE/RMSE (which penalize large errors more), and R-squared (variance explained). Crucially, a single train/test split can be lucky or unlucky, so k-fold cross-validation splits the data into k parts, trains on k-1 and tests on the remaining fold, rotating through, then averages, giving a far more reliable estimate. Underlying all this is the bias-variance tradeoff: underfitting (high bias) means the model is too simple, while overfitting (high variance) means it memorizes noise; good evaluation helps you find the sweet spot.

days.title: Clustering with K-Means
objectives: Apply K-Means and choose the number of clusters
days.beginner_explanation: Clustering is the most common unsupervised learning task, where you group similar data points together without any labels, and K-Means is the classic algorithm for it. K-Means works by choosing k cluster centers, assigning each point to its nearest center, then moving each center to the average of its assigned points, and repeating until the assignments stabilize. Because it relies on distances, you must scale your features first, or a large-range feature will dominate. The tricky part is choosing k, the number of clusters: the elbow method plots the within-cluster error against k and looks for the 'elbow' where adding clusters stops helping much, while the silhouette score measures how well-separated the clusters are. Once you have clusters, the real value comes from interpreting them, profiling each group by its average feature values to give it a meaningful name (for example, 'high-value frequent customers'). Clustering is widely used for customer segmentation, anomaly detection, and exploring structure in data you don't yet understand.
days.practical_task: Scale a dataset's features, run K-Means for several values of k, use the elbow method and silhouette score to pick k, then profile each resulting cluster.

days.title: Dimensionality Reduction with PCA
topics: Using PCA for visualization and preprocessing
days.beginner_explanation: Datasets often have many features, and high dimensionality causes problems: models slow down, overfit, and become hard to visualize, a phenomenon called the curse of dimensionality. Dimensionality reduction compresses many features into a few while keeping most of the information. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is the most widely used technique: it finds new axes, called principal components, that are combinations of the original features ordered by how much variance (spread) they capture. The first component captures the most variation, the second the next most (while being uncorrelated with the first), and so on. By keeping only the top few components you can often represent most of the data's structure in 2 or 3 dimensions, which is invaluable for visualization and for speeding up or de-noising models. You must scale features first, since PCA is sensitive to their variance. The 'explained variance ratio' tells you how much information each component retains, helping you decide how many to keep. PCA is a core tool for exploring and simplifying complex data.

days.mini_project: Build a notebook that uses PCA to visualize a high-dimensional dataset (e.g., digits or a gene-expression set) in 2D, and separately shows how PCA preprocessing affects a downstream model's speed and accuracy.

days.title: Random Forests and Ensemble Methods
objectives: Understand why ensembles outperform single models
topics: Bagging and the Random Forest algorithm
days.beginner_explanation: A single decision tree is easy to understand but prone to overfitting; ensemble methods combine many models to get better, more stable predictions than any one alone. The key insight is that averaging many diverse, individually-imperfect models cancels out their errors. A Random Forest uses a technique called bagging: it trains hundreds of decision trees, each on a random bootstrap sample of the data and each considering only a random subset of features at every split. This randomness makes the trees diverse, and averaging their votes produces a robust model that rarely overfits and works well out of the box on many problems. A handy bonus is the out-of-bag error, a free validation estimate from the data each tree didn't see. Random Forests also compute feature importance, ranking which features drove the predictions, which is valuable for understanding your data. Because they need little tuning and handle mixed data types well, Random Forests are one of the most reliable go-to models for a data scientist's toolkit.
days.practical_task: Train a Random Forest on a classification dataset, compare its accuracy to a single decision tree, extract and plot feature importances, and tune the number of trees and max depth.

quiz.question: What technique does a Random Forest use to create diverse trees?
quiz.answer: Bagging with random feature subsets

quiz.answer: A free validation estimate

days.title: Gradient Boosting Introduction
objectives: Recognize when boosting is the right choice
days.beginner_explanation: Gradient boosting is another ensemble method, but it builds models differently from Random Forests and is often the top performer on structured/tabular data. Where bagging trains many trees independently and averages them, boosting trains trees sequentially, with each new tree focused on correcting the errors (residuals) the previous trees made. This step-by-step error correction, guided by gradient descent on the loss function, produces extremely accurate models. Popular libraries like XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost implement this efficiently and win many data science competitions. The tradeoff is that boosting is more sensitive to hyperparameters than Random Forests: the learning rate controls how much each tree contributes (smaller is more accurate but slower), the number of estimators sets how many trees, and tree depth controls complexity. Because boosting can overfit if you add too many trees, early stopping halts training when validation performance stops improving. As a beginner, knowing when to reach for gradient boosting, and how to tune its core knobs, gives you access to state-of-the-art tabular performance.
days.practical_task: Train an XGBoost or LightGBM model on a tabular dataset, compare it to a Random Forest, tune the learning rate and number of estimators, and use early stopping with a validation set.
days.mini_project: Build a model that applies gradient boosting to a Kaggle-style tabular problem, tunes the main hyperparameters, uses early stopping, and reports how it compares to your earlier Random Forest on the same data.
days.assignment: Experiment with three learning rates and write an explanation of how learning rate trades off accuracy against the number of trees needed.

quiz.answer: It trains trees sequentially, each correcting prior errors

quiz.question: What does early stopping prevent?

quiz.answer: Overfitting from too many boosting rounds

days.title: Building Scikit-learn Pipelines
objectives: Chain preprocessing and modeling into pipelines
days.beginner_explanation: As your workflow grows, doing preprocessing steps manually becomes error-prone, and scikit-learn pipelines solve this by chaining every step, scaling, encoding, and the model, into a single object. This matters most for preventing data leakage, one of the most common and dangerous mistakes in data science: if you fit a scaler or imputer on the entire dataset before splitting, information from the test set leaks into training and inflates your scores, giving a false sense of performance. A pipeline ensures each transformation is fit only on training data within each cross-validation fold. The ColumnTransformer lets you apply different preprocessing to different columns (e.g., scale numerics, one-hot encode categoricals) in one step. Pipelines also make hyperparameter tuning clean: GridSearchCV or RandomizedSearchCV can search over both preprocessing and model parameters together. Finally, you can save the entire fitted pipeline with joblib so it can be reused for deployment. Pipelines turn a messy notebook into reproducible, production-ready, leak-free code.

quiz.answer: It prevents data leakage by fitting preprocessing only on training folds

days.title: Time Series Analysis Basics
objectives: Understand time series data and its components
Work with dates and resampling in Pandas
topics: DateTime indexing and resampling in Pandas
days.beginner_explanation: Time series data is any data indexed by time, such as daily sales, hourly temperatures, or monthly website traffic, and it requires special handling because the order of observations matters and points close in time are related. A time series can be decomposed into components: a trend (long-term direction), seasonality (repeating patterns like weekday or holiday effects), and noise (random fluctuation). Pandas is excellent for this: with a DateTime index you can resample data to different frequencies (e.g., turn daily into monthly), compute rolling-window statistics like a 7-day moving average to smooth noise, and easily slice by date ranges. Two important concepts are stationarity (whether the statistical properties stay constant over time, which many forecasting methods assume) and autocorrelation (how a series correlates with its own past values). Even before advanced models, simple baselines, like predicting tomorrow equals today, or the average of the last week, are surprisingly effective and set the bar any fancier model must beat. Time series skills are essential for forecasting demand, finance, and operations.

days.title: Working with Text Data (NLP Basics)
days.beginner_explanation: A huge amount of real-world data is unstructured text, reviews, tweets, support tickets, and Natural Language Processing (NLP) lets you analyze it. Since models need numbers, the first job is to clean and vectorize text. Cleaning includes lowercasing, removing punctuation and stopwords (common words like 'the' that carry little meaning), and tokenizing (splitting into words). Then you convert text to numbers: the bag-of-words model counts how often each word appears, while TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) weights words by how distinctive they are, downweighting words common across all documents and highlighting rare, informative ones. With these numeric features you can train a standard classifier, for example, to predict whether a review is positive or negative (sentiment analysis). Finally, word embeddings are a more modern approach that represents words as dense vectors capturing meaning, so similar words sit close together, which is the foundation of the deep learning NLP you'd study later. These basics let you turn raw text into insight and predictions.

days.title: Introduction to Deep Learning
objectives: Know when to use deep learning
topics: When to use deep learning vs classical ML
days.beginner_explanation: Deep learning uses neural networks, models loosely inspired by the brain, that excel at complex patterns in images, text, and audio. The building block is a neuron that computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias, and passes the result through a nonlinear activation function (like ReLU) that lets the network learn non-linear relationships. Neurons are organized into layers: an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an output layer, and 'deep' just means many hidden layers. Training happens in two phases: the forward pass computes predictions, and backpropagation calculates how each weight contributed to the error and nudges it in the right direction using gradient descent, repeated over many epochs. Frameworks like Keras make building a network as simple as stacking layers. Because deep networks have many parameters, they overfit easily, so techniques like dropout (randomly disabling neurons during training) help them generalize. Crucially, deep learning isn't always the answer: for typical tabular data, gradient boosting usually wins, while deep learning shines on unstructured data like images and language. Knowing this tradeoff is a mark of a thoughtful data scientist.

days.title: Data Storytelling and Communication
topics: The importance of communication in data science
days.beginner_explanation: The best analysis is worthless if no one understands or acts on it, which is why communication is one of the most valuable and underrated data science skills. Data storytelling means turning your findings into a clear narrative that drives a decision. It starts with knowing your audience: an executive wants the bottom-line implication and recommendation, while a fellow analyst wants the methodology. Effective charts follow principles: choose the right chart type for the message, remove clutter ('chartjunk'), label directly, use color purposefully to highlight rather than decorate, and always start bar-chart axes at zero to avoid exaggerating differences. A good data story has a narrative arc, setting the context, revealing the key insight, and ending with a clear recommendation, rather than dumping every chart you made. You also have an ethical duty to avoid misleading visualizations (truncated axes, cherry-picked ranges, or dual axes that imply false relationships). Mastering communication is often what turns a competent analyst into a trusted, influential data scientist.

days.title: Interactive Dashboards with Plotly
days.beginner_explanation: Static charts tell a fixed story, but interactive visualizations let users explore the data themselves, hovering for details, zooming into regions, and filtering to the subset they care about. Plotly is the leading Python library for this: Plotly Express creates rich, interactive charts in a single line, and every chart supports hover tooltips, zoom, and pan out of the box. When stakeholders need to monitor metrics over time or explore many dimensions, you build a dashboard, a collection of linked visualizations, often with filters and controls, using tools like Plotly Dash or Streamlit. Good dashboards follow design principles: lead with the most important metric, group related charts, keep it uncluttered, and make interactions intuitive. But interactivity isn't always better: for a single clear message, a well-designed static chart is often more effective and less distracting. Learning to build dashboards lets you deliver self-service analytics that stakeholders can use daily without needing you to re-run an analysis each time.

days.title: Version Control and Reproducibility
objectives: Use Git and GitHub for data science projects
days.beginner_explanation: Reproducibility, the ability for you or someone else to re-run your analysis and get the same result, is a cornerstone of trustworthy data science, and version control is how you achieve it. Git is a system that tracks changes to your code over time: you 'commit' snapshots with messages, create 'branches' to try ideas without breaking your main work, and 'merge' them back when ready. GitHub hosts your Git repositories online, enabling backup, collaboration, and a public portfolio that employers can see. Reproducibility also requires capturing your environment: listing exact package versions in a requirements.txt and using virtual environments so your project runs the same on any machine. A professional repository has a clear structure (separate folders for data, notebooks, and source code), a README explaining what the project does and how to run it, and a .gitignore that keeps large data files and secrets out of version control. These practices transform a one-off notebook into a shareable, maintainable project, and they're expected in any real data science job.

days.assignment: Write a README for one of your projects that a stranger could follow to reproduce your results from scratch.

days.beginner_explanation: A model sitting in a notebook helps no one; deployment is how you put it in front of users so it delivers value, and Streamlit is the fastest way for a data scientist to do this without web development experience. The workflow is: train your model, save it to a file with joblib or pickle, then write a Streamlit app, a plain Python script where simple commands like st.slider, st.selectbox, and st.write create an interactive web interface. Your app loads the saved model, collects input from the user through widgets, runs a prediction, and displays the result, all in a browser. Because it's pure Python, you can go from model to working web app in an afternoon. Once it works locally, you can deploy it to Streamlit Community Cloud for free by connecting your GitHub repository, giving you a public URL to share. This is a transformative skill for beginners: it lets you build tangible, shareable data products (a price predictor, a churn calculator, a recommendation demo) that impress employers far more than a static notebook.

days.title: Big Data Concepts and Cloud Basics
objectives: Know the role of cloud platforms in data science
topics: The 3 Vs of big data (volume, velocity, variety)
Data warehouses and lakes at a glance
days.beginner_explanation: Most beginner projects fit comfortably in Pandas on a laptop, but real organizations often have data too large for one machine's memory, and understanding how that's handled is important context even at the beginner level. Big data is often described by the 3 Vs: volume (sheer size), velocity (how fast it arrives), and variety (different formats). When data exceeds a single machine, you use distributed computing, splitting the work across a cluster of machines. The foundational idea is MapReduce (break the problem into pieces, process them in parallel, then combine results), and Apache Spark is the modern engine that does this fast and with a friendly API; PySpark lets you write Spark jobs in Python with DataFrame operations that feel similar to Pandas. All of this typically runs on cloud platforms, AWS, Google Cloud, or Azure, which provide on-demand storage and compute so you don't manage physical servers. You'll also hear about data warehouses (structured, query-optimized stores like BigQuery or Snowflake) and data lakes (raw storage for any format). You don't need to master these as a beginner, but knowing the landscape helps you understand how data science scales in industry.

days.assignment: Write a one-page summary explaining, in your own words, when you would move from Pandas to Spark and what a data warehouse vs a data lake is used for.

days.title: Ethics, Bias, and Responsible Data Science
objectives: Recognize bias and fairness issues in data and models
Apply responsible practices to your work
topics: Sources of bias in data and models
Privacy, consent, and data protection (GDPR basics)
days.beginner_explanation: Data science has real power over people's lives, deciding who gets a loan, a job interview, or medical attention, so doing it responsibly is not optional. A central concern is bias: models learn from historical data, and if that data reflects past discrimination, the model will reproduce and even amplify it, producing unfair outcomes for certain groups. This is why you check for disparate impact (whether a model performs differently across groups defined by protected attributes like gender or race) using fairness metrics, not just overall accuracy. Privacy is another duty: you must handle personal data lawfully and ethically, respecting consent and regulations like GDPR, minimizing what you collect, and protecting it. Transparency matters too, stakeholders and affected people deserve to understand how a model makes decisions, which is why interpretability tools are important, especially in high-stakes settings. Responsible practice means questioning your data's origins, testing for harm before deployment, documenting limitations, and being willing to say a model shouldn't be built. Building this mindset early makes you not just a skilled data scientist but a trustworthy one.

days.mini_project: Conduct a fairness audit of a model on a dataset with a sensitive attribute: measure group-wise performance, identify any disparate impact, and propose concrete mitigation steps.
days.assignment: Find a real-world case where a data/AI system caused harm through bias or privacy failure and write a short analysis of what went wrong and how it could have been prevented.

days.title: A/B Testing and Business Metrics
topics: What A/B testing is and why companies use it
Designing an experiment (hypothesis, metric, sample size)
days.beginner_explanation: A/B testing is how data-driven companies make decisions: instead of guessing whether a new button, price, or feature is better, they run a controlled experiment. Users are randomly split into a control group (current version, A) and a treatment group (new version, B), and you compare a chosen metric between them. This is the practical application of the hypothesis testing you learned: the null hypothesis is 'no difference,' and you use a statistical test to decide whether the observed difference is real or just chance. Good design comes first: you define one primary metric tied to a business goal (like conversion rate or revenue per user), and you calculate the required sample size in advance so the test has enough power to detect a meaningful effect. Connecting to business metrics is key, a data scientist must translate statistical results into business impact ('this change would lift revenue ~3%'). Beware common pitfalls: 'peeking' at results early and stopping when they look good inflates false positives, testing many metrics at once causes spurious findings, and short-term 'novelty effects' can mislead. A/B testing is one of the most valued and widely-used skills in industry data science.
days.practical_task: Design an A/B test for a hypothetical product change: state the hypothesis and primary metric, calculate the needed sample size, then simulate data and run the correct statistical test to reach a conclusion.
days.mini_project: Analyze a real or simulated A/B test dataset end to end: check the setup, run the appropriate test, compute the effect size and confidence interval, and write a business-facing recommendation.
days.assignment: Explain, with an example, why stopping an A/B test as soon as it looks significant ('peeking') leads to false positives.

quiz.question: Why do you calculate sample size before running a test?

quiz.answer: To ensure enough power to detect a meaningful effect

days.title: The Data Science Workflow End-to-End
objectives: Integrate all skills into a coherent workflow
Frame a business problem as a data problem

days.beginner_explanation: Having learned many individual skills, this day steps back to connect them into the single coherent workflow that professional data scientists follow, so the capstone doesn't feel like a jumble of techniques. Revisiting the CRISP-DM framework (Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modeling, Evaluation, Deployment) now means something concrete because you have tools for every stage. The most underrated skill is the very first step: translating a fuzzy business question ('why are customers leaving?') into a precise, answerable data question ('can we predict which customers will churn next month, and what factors drive it?'), and defining what success looks like in measurable terms. From there you consciously choose methods for each stage, EDA and statistics to understand, feature engineering to prepare, the right model for the task, proper evaluation metrics, and a deployment approach. Just as important is managing scope: real projects iterate, start with a simple baseline, learn from it, and improve, rather than trying to build everything at once. This day is where you plan your capstone: pick a problem you care about, define the question and success metric, and sketch the workflow you'll execute over the next days.

days.title: Capstone Part 1: Data Acquisition and EDA
objectives: Acquire and understand your capstone data
days.beginner_explanation: The capstone is where everything comes together into one portfolio-worthy project, and Part 1 focuses on the foundation that determines whether the whole project succeeds: understanding and preparing your data. You start by acquiring your chosen dataset, whether from a source like Kaggle, a public API, or your own web scraping, and loading it reliably. Then you conduct a comprehensive EDA: examine every variable's distribution, understand relationships and correlations, identify missing values, outliers, and data quality issues, and, crucially, write down the insights and hypotheses you discover, because this analysis will guide your modeling choices. Next you clean and prepare the data: handle missing values thoughtfully, fix inconsistencies, and engineer initial features. Throughout, you document your data understanding clearly, this is what turns a notebook into a professional deliverable a reader can follow. Real practitioners spend the majority of their time on exactly this phase, because a model built on poorly understood, messy data will fail no matter how sophisticated the algorithm. By the end of Part 1 you should deeply understand your data and have a clean dataset ready for modeling.

days.title: Capstone Part 2: Modeling and Evaluation
objectives: Build and compare models for your capstone
Tune and validate your best model
topics: Rigorous evaluation with the right metrics
days.beginner_explanation: With clean, well-understood data from Part 1, Part 2 is where you build the predictive or analytical heart of your capstone. Follow the disciplined workflow you learned: start with a simple baseline model (like logistic or linear regression) so you have something to beat and a sanity check. Then train several candidate models, perhaps a Random Forest and a gradient boosting model, inside proper pipelines to prevent data leakage, and compare them using cross-validation for reliable estimates. Tune your most promising model's hyperparameters with grid or randomized search. Evaluate rigorously with the metrics appropriate to your problem (F1 or ROC-AUC for imbalanced classification, RMSE or MAE for regression), not just accuracy, and look at where the model makes mistakes via a confusion matrix or error analysis. Finally, interpret the model: use feature importances or coefficients to explain what drives predictions, connecting back to the hypotheses from your EDA. Honesty is essential, report the real performance and the model's limitations rather than overselling. By the end of Part 2 you have a validated, interpretable model and a clear story about what it can and cannot do.

This combination, a live deployed app, a clear written report, and a crisp presentation, is exactly what turns a learning project into a portfolio centerpiece that showcases the full range of data science skills to potential employers.

days.title: Building Your Data Science Portfolio
topics: What makes a strong DS portfolio
Writing project READMEs and case studies
days.beginner_explanation: Skills only get you hired if others can see them, and a portfolio is how a beginner data scientist proves capability without prior job experience. A strong portfolio isn't a pile of tutorials redone; it's a small set of two to four polished projects that each tell a clear story: a real problem, your approach, and a concrete result, ideally with at least one deployed app (like your capstone) that a recruiter can click and use. For each project you write a clear README or case study that frames the business question, summarizes your method, highlights key findings with a visual or two, and links to the live demo, because hiring managers skim and need the value in seconds. Your GitHub profile becomes your professional home: pin your best repositories, keep commit histories clean, and write a profile README introducing yourself. Optionally, writing short blog posts about your projects or participating in Kaggle demonstrates communication skills and initiative. The goal is that within a few minutes, anyone can see that you can take a problem from raw data all the way to a working, well-communicated solution, which is exactly what employers want to verify.

days.title: Career Preparation and Next Steps
topics: Practicing SQL, ML, and behavioral questions
days.beginner_explanation: The final day turns your new skills into a job search strategy and a plan for continued growth. First, understand the landscape: 'data science' spans several roles, data analyst (focused on SQL, dashboards, and business insight), data scientist (statistics, ML, and experimentation), ML engineer (productionizing models), and data engineer (building data pipelines), and knowing which fits your interests helps you target the right jobs and tailor your resume. Your resume and LinkedIn should emphasize projects and measurable results, not just a list of tools, and link to your portfolio and GitHub. Interview preparation is crucial because DS interviews are broad: expect SQL query challenges, statistics and probability questions, ML concept questions (like explaining the bias-variance tradeoff or how a Random Forest works), a take-home or case study where you analyze a problem, and behavioral questions about how you work. Practicing these deliberately, using platforms for SQL and ML questions and mock interviews, makes a big difference. Finally, data science is a field of continuous learning: plan your next steps, deepen a specialization, take on harder projects, contribute to open source, and keep learning, so this 45-day roadmap becomes the launchpad for a long, evolving career.
